caveats: a: The occupation source spans all local government functions and is not specific to combined executive-legislative offices.
a: Federal AI adoption is only directional evidence for state and local offices.
a: The frozen labor-share input is missing because government activity has no compatible receipts denominator.
rho: No implementation-rate evidence specific to NAICS 921140 was found.
rho: Federal agency experience may overstate technical resources and understate the speed of small-jurisdiction deployment through commodity software.
e: Private technology, consulting, staffing, and records vendors serving these offices are classified outside NAICS 921140.
e: The frozen dataset explicitly provides no defensible LMM firm count.
s5: Political succession is not ownership succession.
s5: Government consolidation or dissolution does not create an acquirable firm.
q: Public budget retention is not equivalent to EBITDA benefit retained after customer pass-through.
q: Assigning a neutral value would conceal the construct's inapplicability.
d5: Government-unit counts are not service quantity and include governments whose executive and legislative offices are separately classified.
d5: The range excludes changes in service quality, meeting complexity, and constituent expectations that counts cannot reveal.
o: This is a bounded institutional judgment, not an observed operator-share statistic.
o: A high operator requirement does not imply a private acquisition opportunity.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: These offices exercise public authority for residents and constituents and are funded through appropriated government budgets and public revenues rather than customer contracts, service fees, or normalized EBITDA. Their output includes deliberation, ordinances, policy decisions, hearings, constituent communication, agendas, records, and executive oversight.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default outsourced-service LMM lens cannot be populated: Census defines the industry as government establishments, and the frozen data provide neither a receipts concept nor an LMM firm count. The full industry is retained only to make that structural mismatch explicit, not narrowed to a favorable private analogue.
lens.excluded_activities: Separate executive offices, separate legislative bodies, private government contractors classified in other industries, public agencies outside this code, financing vehicles, shells, and any activity without combined executive-legislative authority.
lens.included_activities: Government establishments serving as combined executive and legislative councils, boards of commissioners, or boards of supervisors are retained for operational analysis; no commercial lower-middle-market firm population is included because the NAICS definition is exclusively governmental.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation mix of all local government excluding schools and hospitals, not wage-weighted tasks in NAICS 921140. That broad population has 17.44% office and administrative support, 5.61% management, 5.32% business and financial operations, and 1.79% legal employment, while combined executive-legislative offices should be more concentrated in management, policy, legal, communications, and administrative work than local government overall. I therefore raise exposure relative to the broad mix but retain a wide range. This may overstate substitution because elected judgment, public meetings, negotiation, and legal accountability are resistant, or understate it where small offices still rely on manual document and constituent workflows.
caveats: The occupation source spans all local government functions and is not specific to combined executive-legislative offices.
Federal AI adoption is only directional evidence for state and local offices.
The frozen labor-share input is missing because government activity has no compatible receipts denominator.
a.change_evidence: A 921140-specific payroll-weighted task census covering elected officials, administrators, clerks, analysts, attorneys, and communications staff, with current automation and hours by task, would materially change this range.
a.rationale: Drafting, summarization, agenda and minutes preparation, records search, policy comparison, budget analysis, scheduling, translation, and constituent triage are exposed. Deliberation, political judgment, lawful votes, public representation, negotiation, and accountable decisions are not. BLS expects AI-driven efficiency in office support work [S3], and GAO reports rapid growth in federal AI use cases [S4].

d5.bridge: Census counts general-purpose local governments, not constant-price, constant-quality output of combined executive-legislative offices. Their number was nearly unchanged, moving from 38,736 in 2022 to 38,704 in 2025, and total government counts have remained near 90,000 since 2007. I translate this institutional stability into roughly flat five-year demand for the current governance basket. The proxy may understate workload growth from population, regulation, and constituent expectations, or overstate staffing demand where digital service and consolidation reduce work per jurisdiction.
caveats: Government-unit counts are not service quantity and include governments whose executive and legislative offices are separately classified.
The range excludes changes in service quality, meeting complexity, and constituent expectations that counts cannot reveal.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year NAICS 921140 real expenditures, employment hours, meeting and legislative workload, constituent case volumes, and jurisdiction consolidations would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Combined councils and boards are foundational organs of general-purpose government, so jurisdictional persistence is a useful directional demand indicator. Census reports a stable count of general-purpose governments from 2022 to 2025 [S5].

caveats: Private technology, consulting, staffing, and records vendors serving these offices are classified outside NAICS 921140.
The frozen dataset explicitly provides no defensible LMM firm count.
e.change_evidence: Only a redefinition of the screened population to include public entities, or a different NAICS code covering private outsourced government-support contractors, would make this primitive measurable.
e.rationale: There is no LMM-firm denominator and no eligible commercial firm population: the industry consists of government establishments without normalized EBITDA or transferable private control. A numeric eligible share would imply a commercial population that the code does not contain.

l.detail: No receipts concept for this code (government / SUSB out of scope). QCEW government wages exist ($121,662,349,884) but there is no revenue denominator; labor_share left null.
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0000 (margins.json[92]: ESTIMATE: public administration — EBITDA is not meaningful for government units; margin 0 makes the n_band chain yield 0 by construction (recorded as a known gap)); revenue/employee $374,583 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at --); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: This is a bounded institutional judgment, not an observed operator-share statistic.
A high operator requirement does not imply a private acquisition opportunity.
o.change_evidence: Changes in state law authorizing automated binding decisions, sustained consolidation of general-purpose governments, or evidence that material current council functions disappear rather than become more efficient would change the estimate.
o.rationale: Nearly all year-five governance quantity still requires an accountable public body to deliberate, vote, exercise delegated authority, maintain lawful records, and answer to constituents. Software may eliminate clerical steps and enable self-service information access, but it cannot itself hold office, constitute a council, lawfully vote, or bear public accountability.

caveats: Public budget retention is not equivalent to EBITDA benefit retained after customer pass-through.
Assigning a neutral value would conceal the construct's inapplicability.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level pricing and benefit-sharing evidence would become relevant only for private vendors in a different NAICS industry and a redefined lens.
q.rationale: There is no commercial operator, customer price, renewal repricing, or investor-retained gross benefit in the frozen lens. Budget savings and service improvements may be socially valuable but are not commercial value retention as defined by this primitive.

caveats: No implementation-rate evidence specific to NAICS 921140 was found.
Federal agency experience may overstate technical resources and understate the speed of small-jurisdiction deployment through commodity software.
rho.change_evidence: A panel of municipal and county combined offices reporting tool adoption, production use, reviewed error rates, staff hours saved, procurement lead times, and governance controls over five years would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Document, search, meeting-support, translation, intake, and analytical tools can be deployed within five years, but implementation is limited by public-records duties, procurement cycles, fragmented legacy systems, cybersecurity, privacy, accessibility, model error, political scrutiny, and the need for human review. GAO found both rapid federal use-case growth and recurring policy, budget, technical-resource, and privacy obstacles [S4].

caveats: Political succession is not ownership succession.
Government consolidation or dissolution does not create an acquirable firm.
s5.change_evidence: No ordinary diligence evidence can make this construct applicable while the lens remains private control transfers; applicability would require an explicit methodology change.
s5.rationale: Government offices do not undergo qualifying private control transfers. Elections, appointments, reorganizations, consolidations, and changes in officeholders are not acquisitions of transferable LMM operating companies and cannot be translated into this primitive.

principal_driver: Document-intensive administrative and analytical workflows offer tangible public-sector AI efficiency opportunities.
principal_weakness: The industry contains government offices, not transferable LMM service firms, making eligibility, control transfer, and commercial retention inapplicable.

sources.supports: Defines 921140 as government establishments serving as councils and boards where the chief executive is a member of the legislative body.
sources.title: NAICS Sector 92 Definitions: 921140 Executive and Legislative Offices, Combined

sources.supports: Provides the broad local-government occupation mix, including 17.44% office and administrative support, 5.61% management, 5.32% business and financial operations, and 1.79% legal employment.
sources.title: Local Government, excluding Schools and Hospitals: May 2023 OEWS

sources.supports: States that AI integration is expected to create additional efficiency gains in office and administrative support work and constrain demand for several clerical occupations.
sources.title: Industry and Occupational Employment Projections Overview and Highlights, 2024–34

sources.supports: Reports that selected agencies' AI use cases nearly doubled from 2023 to 2024 and generative-AI cases rose about ninefold, while agencies cited policy, privacy, budget, technical-resource, and governance obstacles.
sources.title: Generative AI Use and Management at Federal Agencies

sources.supports: Reports that general-purpose local governments numbered 38,736 in 2022 and 38,704 in 2025 and that total government counts have remained near 90,000 since 2007.
sources.title: Government Organization Summary Report: 2022 and 2025

title: Executive and Legislative Offices, Combined
